sections.about: This metric measures the percentage of workers trained on skill upgradation under BRSR Principle 3, Essential Indicator 8. Skill-upgradation training can include internal training and external education support; it supports productivity, risk management and adoption of new methods. A higher value indicates better performance.
sections.performanceAndDrivers: Tata and JSW reported 100% worker skill-upgradation coverage in all three years. Jindal reported 100% in FY2022-23, fell to 3.73% in FY2023-24, and recovered to 100% in FY2024-25. SAIL did not report worker skill-upgradation coverage in any of the three years. This is the clearest differentiation point in the training-development theme: SAIL appears strong on worker H&S training but weak on BRSR disclosure of worker skill upgradation, whereas Tata’s disclosed system covers both safety and skills at full scale. SAIL’s FY2023-24 BRSR explicitly marked worker skill-upgradation training as “NA - Not Available,” even though worker H&S training was 100%. Jindal’s recovery aligns with its FY2024-25 disclosure that workers receive mandatory and need-based training, including behavioural safety, quality management, environment and sustainability, and regular e-learning modules for skill upgradation.
sections.tataPositioning: In FY2024-25, Tata Steel is Rank 1 at 100%, tied with JSW and Jindal. The gap from best performer is 0 percentage points; SAIL is ranked last because the value is not reported. Tata’s ranking has remained stable at Rank 1 across all three years, which is important because worker capability is directly linked to shop-floor reliability, permit-to-work discipline, crane and mobile equipment safety, refractory shutdown practices, process stability, and contractor productivity.
sections.comparabilityNotes: SAIL’s worker skill-upgradation coverage is not reported across all three years. Tata Steel FY2022-23 is consolidated.
